segments.s728-g97: همی‌تاخت is an older/poetic continuous past form (همی + past stem) meaning 'was charging/was rushing' — it marks an ongoing past action. تا here introduces the endpoint 'up to/until' (prepositional sense), so the verb is followed by its directional complement بیشه نارون ('the elm grove'). Word order in poetry places the verb before the endpoint.
segments.s728-g98: This is a nominal phrase + prepositional phrase. فرستاده functions as a noun 'the messenger' (the ezāfe genitive relation with the following زن is implicit: 'فرستادهٔ زن' = 'the woman's messenger'), and به نزدیک زن is a prepositional phrase ('to/near the woman') indicating direction/location. Poetry often omits linking vowels, so the possession is shown by adjacency rather than an overt -e.
segments.s728-g99: ازو is the prepositional pronoun 'from her/of her.' گردیه شد is a compound where گردیه (a verbal/adjectival form) + شد ('became/occurred') expresses that something 'turned/appeared' or 'became turned' — a poetic intransitive result. چو introduces a simile ('like/as'), and خرم بهار is the simile's noun phrase 'joyful/luscious spring.' The clause structure is: [from her] + [it turned/became] + [like X].
segments.s728-g100: همان رخ = 'that very face' (demonstrative + noun). پر از is the adjectival/complementive construction 'full of' followed by a coordinated list of nouns (بوی و رنگ و نگار). The coordinating و links the qualities 'scent, color, and beauty/adorning' that fill the face.
segments.s728-g101: زبهرام is a prepositional/agent phrase 'from Bahram' or 'concerning Bahram' that sets the topic or source. چندی is an adverb meaning 'for a while' or 'a little while.' سخن is the object 'words/speech' and راندند is a past plural verb 'they drove' used idiomatically here with سخن to mean 'they uttered/spoke' — Persian poetic usage can pair راندن with سخن to mean 'to speak/utter.'
segments.s728-g102: همی is a poetic particle that signals continuous or repeated action ('kept on/continued to'). آب مژگان is an ezāfe-less genitive phrase 'the water of the eyelashes' (a common poetic image for tears). بر + افشاندند is a verb phrase where بر indicates motion/over and افشاندند 'they scattered/sprinkled,' so together 'they shed/sprinkled (tears).' The verb is past plural, coordinated with the previous clause's subject.
segments.s728-g103: پس is a temporal/discourse adverb 'then/afterwards.' آن نامه is 'that letter.' شوی is from the verb شدن used here as a stative/auxiliary meaning roughly 'being/was' (poetic usage to mark the state of the letter); with با خط شاه ('with the king's hand/script') it specifies that the letter was in/with the king's handwriting. In short: 'that letter, being in the king's hand/script.'
segments.s728-g104: نهانی is an adverb 'secretly/covertly' modifying the verb داد ('gave'). بدو is the enclitic/compound pronoun 'to him/unto it' as the indirect object. داد is the past tense 'gave.' The conjunction و links the following clause بنمود راه where بنمود is past 'showed' (he/she showed) and راه is the object 'the way/path' — 'and (she) showed the way.'
segments.s728-g89: چو is a classical conjunction meaning 'when' (temporal) introducing a subordinate temporal clause. The clause's basic order is Subject (جفت من) + Verb (آید) + adverbial/PP (به نزدیک تو); جفت من ('my mate') is the subject and آید is the 3rd sg. present/imperative narrative 'comes' used in classical narration.
segments.s728-g90: درخشان (adjective) precedes the verb کند ('makes/does') and functions as the predicative result (i.e. 'make ... bright'). جان تاریک تو is the direct object phrase (جان 'soul' modified by تاریک 'dark' and the possessive تو after the noun). کند is 3rd sg. present/past-form used as the causative/active verb 'makes'. Note the poetic omission of the object marker را.
segments.s728-g91: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' and introduces the source (گفتار او = 'his words'). هیچ‌گونه ('in no way/at all') is an adverbial negation intensifier modifying the verb that follows. مگرد is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'do not turn aside / do not depart' (negative imperative); this older form is outside the provided sense list, so it behaves as an imperative prohibition addressed to the addressee.
segments.s728-g92: چو again introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if'. گردی is used as a noun-image 'dust' (i.e. 'as dust' / 'like dust') and شود is 3rd sg. present/past-form of شدن 'become'. بخت را is the definite object/topic 'fortune' marked by را (here used idiomatically with compound expression), and روی زرد ('face yellow/pale') is a predicate where زرد is 'pale/wan' rather than literal yellow. The clause reads 'when, as dust, fortune's face becomes pale.'
segments.s728-g93: نهاد is the classical past 3rd sg. of نهادن 'placed/put'. The direct object آن خط خسرو ('that line/inscription of the king' or 'that royal writing') precedes the verb (word order is flexible in classical Persian). اندر میان is a locative phrase 'in the midst / in the middle'.
segments.s728-g94: بپیچید is a past-form of پیچیدن with the prefix ب- (here 'wrapped/rolled/twisted'). برنامه is the object ('plan/arrangement' or possibly 'the written matter'), and بر پرنیان is a prepositional phrase 'upon/in/around silk' specifying how it was wrapped. Classical Persian often omits the object marker را in such poetic lines.
segments.s728-g95: زن چاره‌گر is the subject noun phrase 'the resourceful/clever woman' (compound modifier follows the noun in classical style). بستد is past 3rd sg. of بستن 'bound/tied/sealed', and آن نامه را is the definite object 'that letter' marked by را.
segments.s728-g96: شنید is past 3rd sg. 'she heard'. The object is آن سخنهای خود کامه را: سخنهای 'the words' modified by خود کامه (an idiomatic compound meaning 'that which pleases/fulfills her' or 'her desired/pleasing words'), and را marks the object. Grammatically, خود intensifies/possessivizes (her own/the very) and کامه describes the words as satisfying or desired.
segments.s728-g81: 'سرنامه گفت' is a conventional introductory phrase (literally “the chronicle said”); 'آنک' is a classical conjunctive pronoun introducing the content clause (≈ 'that' / 'which'); 'بهرام کرد' is a simple past clause: subject + past verb ('Bahram did'). The past tense verb 'کرد' at the end is the main verb of the reported clause.
segments.s728-g82: The phrase lists the result of Bahram's action: 'همه' ('all/everything') distributes over the coordinated nouns 'دوده و بوم' ('soot and land'), and the adjective 'بدنام' ('disgraced/notorious') sits before the final verb 'کرد' which predicates the whole phrase. In other words, the verb 'کرد' applies to both the soot-smearing and the dishonouring of the land.
segments.s728-g83: 'که' here introduces a wish/optative clause (equivalent to 'may...'); 'بخشایش' is the noun 'forgiveness' and 'آراد یزدان' is an epithet for the deity ('آراد' = noble/pure, 'یزدان' = God). 'بروی' in this poetic clause functions as a jussive/optative sense ('be upon you' / 'may it come upon you') — an idiomatic older usage rather than the ordinary verb 'to go'.
segments.s728-g84: 'مبادا' is a negative hortatory/precautionary particle meaning 'may it not be' / 'lest'; it governs the predicate 'پشیمان' ('regretful'). The prepositional phrase 'ازان گفت وگوی' ('of that conversation') names the thing one should not regret; 'گفت وگوی' is the older compound 'گفت‌وگو' (conversation) split into two poetic parts.
segments.s728-g85: 'هرآنکس' ('whoever') begins the relative clause introduced by 'که'. 'جانش ندارد' is literally 'does not have his life/heart' (جانش = his life/spirit), with 'ندارد' as the negative present/presentive verb 'has not'; 'خرد' (wisdom) follows as the thing lacking — a typical poetic word order where the possessed thing (جان) and the negation precede the noun that completes the thought ('he lacks wisdom').
segments.s728-g86: 'کم و بیشی' is a fixed paired phrase meaning 'less and more' / 'small or great'; it modifies 'کارها' ('affairs, matters'). The verb 'ننگرد' (negative form of 'نگریستن') means 'does not regard/consider' (third-person singular), so the clause says that such a person will not concern himself with matters, whether small or large.
segments.s728-g87: 'گر' is the conditional 'if'. 'او رفت' is a simple past clause 'he went/if he goes', and the following 'ما از پس اورویم' uses 'از پس' ('after') + verb with first-person plural ending to mean 'we will follow after him' — the object pronoun is incorporated in the verb ending in older poetic forms, so word order is compact and slightly archaic.
segments.s728-g88: This short clause names to whom they will turn: 'خدای جهان' = 'the God of the world'. 'بداد' at the start is an older/poetic word whose precise nuance here is 'with regard to justice/relief' (the line means they will appeal to God). 'بگرویم' is a jussive/future-form verb ('we will turn to / we will resort to'); the verb is in the same compound poetic register.
segments.s728-g57: This clause uses خواهم (future auxiliary) + object to state intent: 'I will (خواهم) [do X]'. یکی رقعه = 'one/a single note' (numeric + noun). برو here is an archaic spelling of بر (on/with) governing مهر شاه 'the king's seal' — the phrase means 'on/with the king's seal.' Word order is subject omitted (understood 'I') + object + verb phrase (خواهم + بر[=on] + مهر شاه).
segments.s728-g58: همان here emphasizes 'the very same' or 'that same.' خط او = 'his handwriting' (noun + possessive pronoun او). چون = 'like/as' introducing a simile, followed by the adjective درخشنده and noun ماه 'moon.' The clause is a predicative comment on the previous clause: 'that handwriting of his is like the shining moon.'
segments.s728-g59: به + noun (به خوارهر) marks direction/recipient 'to that contemptible one.' فرستم is 1st-person present/future 'I send/I will send.' زن خویش را = 'my/one's own wife' (زن + خوی + possessive ش; را marks the definite object). The clause states the sending action and its object.
segments.s728-g60: کنم is the 1st-person singular subjunctive/form of کردن used to express 'I will make/do' (here causative/performative: 'I will cause / I will put'). دور = 'far/away'; زین در should be read as 'ز این در' (archaic/compacted) meaning 'from this court/door' (so a locative), and بد اندیش را = 'the ill-intentioned one' with را marking the object. Together: 'I will make (him) far from this court' = 'I will drive the ill-intentioned man away.'
segments.s728-g61: که begins a clause giving a reason or explanation ('for' / 'that'). چونین سخن = 'such talk' (demonstrative + noun). نیست جز = 'is nothing except' (negative copula نیست + exception جز), and کار زن = 'a woman's affair' (کار functioning as 'affair/matter'). Word order follows classical Persian: topic then copula/negation then exception.
segments.s728-g62: به ویژه = 'especially/particularly.' زنی = 'a woman' (indefinite). کو here is an archaic relative meaning 'who' (introducing the relative clause), بود = past copula 'was,' رای زن = 'the opinion/counsel of women' (رای + genitive noun). So the relative clause means 'especially a woman who had the (typical) opinion of women.'
segments.s728-g63: برین is an orthographic contraction of بر این ('upon/at this'); نیز = 'also'; هر چون ... همی‌بنگرم = 'whenever/as I look' (هر چون = 'whenever' or 'each time that'; همی‌بنگرم is the present habitual/formal 'I look' or 'I behold'). The whole clause means 'and each time I look at this.'
segments.s728-g64: پیام تو = 'your message' (noun + pronoun). باید = 'must/ought' expressing obligation. بر here means 'to/toward' (directional), and خواهرم = 'my sister' (noun + possessive). The clause means 'your message should be delivered to my sister.'
segments.s728-g65: بر آید is a verbal phrase meaning 'come about / turn out' with بر (literally 'upon') prefixed to آید; here آید is used as a present/jussive with a wish-like force ('may it come'). بکام تو ('to your desire') is the dative/benefactive phrase that follows the verb. این کار زود ('this matter soon') is fronted for emphasis, so the temporal adverb زود attaches to the whole clause.
segments.s728-g66: برین is a fused form of بر + این ('upon/with regard to this'). بیش و کم ('more and less') names excess and deficiency; نباید + verb (فزود) is an impersonal prohibition 'one should not.../it ought not to...'; فزود (from فزاییدن) is the verb 'increase/add'. The literal sense: 'On this one ought not to increase (neither more nor less)'.
segments.s728-g67: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بشنید is the past 'he heard'; خسرو is the subject. بران ('بر آن', 'about that') specifies what he heard. شاد شد is the resulting predicate 'became happy' (شد = 'became'). Word order is typical poetic inversion but grammar is straightforward: temporal clause → past verb → result.
segments.s728-g68: همه رنجها ('all the pains/sufferings') is the subject phrase; بر دلش ('upon his heart') is a locative expression specifying where they lay. باد شد here is idiomatic: باد ('wind') + شد ('became') yields the image 'were blown away/vanished'. The construction thus means 'all his sufferings were blown from his heart.'
segments.s728-g69: هم آنگه ('also then') is an adverbial sequence marker. ز گنجور قرطاس خواست is 'he asked for/desired paper from the treasury' — ز introduces the source ('from the treasury'), قرطاس is the thing requested (paper), and خواست is the past-tense verb 'he asked/wanted'.
segments.s728-g70: This clause has ز marking source/ablative ('from') followed by a noun cluster مشک سیه سوده which describes the source/item ('musk, black, Sudeh' — Sudeh here appears as a name or label). انقاس is the direct object of خواست (the thing he requested) and خواست is again the past verb 'he requested'. The exact sense of انقاس is obscure/archaic in this line (see senses map).
segments.s728-g71: یکی here is indefinite ('a certain' / 'one'); نامه is the direct object 'letter'. بنوشت is a poetic/past form of 'نوشت' ('he wrote'); the following چون بوستان begins a simile 'like a garden'. The verb is placed before/around the object in this poetic ordering.
segments.s728-g72: This is a paired simile describing the letter's content: گل بوستان ('the flowers of the garden') is the first noun phrase, and چون رخ دوستان ('like the faces of friends') completes the comparison. The verb is omitted (a common poetic ellipsis), so read as '(are) like a garden's flowers, like the faces of friends.'
segments.s728-g129: پس is a temporal/conclusive particle meaning "then." آن نامه is the direct object ('that letter') placed before the subject (typical of poetic word order). شاه is the subject ('the king'). بنمودشان is the past tense (classical) of نمودن/نمود (to show); the enclitic -شان is a 3rd‑person plural object/benefactive pronoun ('to them'/'for them'), so the whole clause reads 'then the king showed them that letter.'
segments.s728-g130: This is a short coordinated clause with the noun phrase 'دلیری و تندی' ('courage/bravery and swiftness/haste') followed by the verb بی‌فزودشان. In classical Persian بیفزود is the past of افزودن (here with the prosthetic/older prefix بی‑) meaning 'added/increased'; the enclitic -شان again marks 'to them.' The conjunction و simply links the two nouns being increased.
segments.s728-g131: همه ('all/everyone') is a universal quantifier modifying the following plural agent. سرکشان is a plural/collective form meaning 'the headstrong/rebels' (an irregular plural of سرکش in older/poetic usage). آفرین here is an exclamation/acclamation ('bravo'/'well done') and خواندند is past tense 'they uttered/read/sang' (3rd person plural), so the clause means 'all the headstrong ones cried "Afarin!"'.
segments.s728-g132: This clause is ordered with prepositional/locative elements before the verb, a common poetic order: بران نامه ('on/onto that letter' — بر + آن نامه contracted to بران نامه) identifies the target, برگوهر ('with/on jewels' — بر + گوهر) marks what is being scattered, and افشاندند is the past tense verb 'they scattered' (3rd person plural). Together: 'they scattered jewels upon that letter.'
segments.s728-g121: سپهبد is the subject. به تاریکی اندر is a locative phrase ('into/into the darkness') built with the preposition به plus the noun تاریکی and the older locative/adhesive اندر; the verb بمرد (classical form with the b- prefix) functions here as the past/poetic verb 'died'. Word order is Subject — adverbial locative — verb.
segments.s728-g122: شب و روز روشن is a temporal/adverbial phrase 'night and (the) clear/brilliant day' (the adjective روشن qualifies روز). به خسرو is the dative 'to Khosrow', and سپرد is the past verb 'handed/entrusted (over)'. Together they say that (the body/person) was delivered to Khosrow when day became clear.
segments.s728-g123: بشهر اندرون is a doubled locative 'in/within the city' (بـ + شهر together with اندرون). بانگ و فریاد is a coordinated noun phrase 'cry and outcry/shout', and خاست is the archaic past 'arose' (خاستن). The clause therefore means 'cries and shouts arose within the city.'
segments.s728-g124: بهر بر زنی is a directional phrase: بهر (toward/for) + بر (on/against) + زنی (a woman) — together indicating action aimed at the woman. آتش وباد خاست is a compound subject 'fire and (the) wind arose' with وباد here representing 'and wind' fused as one poetic token; خاست again is the past 'arose'.
segments.s728-g125: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آواز بشنید is 'heard the voice/call' (آواز object, بشنید past 'heard'). ناباک زن is a noun phrase modifying/identifying that woman; here ناباک is an adjectival epithet attached to زن. The whole clause is a temporal subordinate: 'When the (named) woman heard the voice…'.
segments.s728-g126: بخفتان is an infinitival/poetic form related to sleeping ('for sleeping' / 'to lie down') that frames purpose/context. رومی qualifies the garment as 'Roman/Byzantine'. بپوشید is the past/poetic form 'put on, clothed', and تن ('body') is the direct object — 'she covered/dressed her body in a Roman sleeping-garment.' The verb carries the classical b- prefix used in older narratives.
segments.s728-g127: شب تیره is a temporal phrase 'the dark night'. ایرانیان را is the object with the accusative marker را attached (here written as part of the verb: رابخواند), and خواند (in the fused form رابخواند) is the past verb 'called/summoned' — so the clause means 'he summoned the Iranians on the dark night.'
segments.s728-g128: سخنهای آن کشته is a noun phrase 'the accounts/tales of those slain' (سخنهای = 'words/tales', آن کشته = 'those killed/slain'). چندی 'for a while/several (moments)' is an adverbial, and براند here functions as the past action-verb coordinating with the previous clause (in context: 'he spoke/told for a while about the slain'); in older usage the verb form is poetic. (Note: the printed dictionary senses offered for براند are 'to drive', but in this narrative context it functions as the clause's action-verb meaning 'uttered/put forward' in a poetic register.)
segments.s728-g25: کنون (adverb) is fronted for emphasis: 'now / at present'. چاره‌ای هست is a copular clause ('a remedy/solution exists'); هست is the copula/existential verb. نزدیک من is a predicative/adverbial phrase (‘near me’), placed after the copula as its complement.
segments.s728-g26: مگو is the classical negative imperative (‘do not say’), directed at the addressee. این سخن is the direct object ('this speech/this matter'). بر سر انجمن is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before/in front of the assembly' (بر + سر used idiomatically with انجمن).
segments.s728-g27: سوی is a directional preposition 'toward/to' introducing the indirect object. گردیه functions as an adjective or noun meaning 'assembled/gathered' (the assembled people). نامه is the direct object 'letter'. باید is the modal 'must/ought', followed by the bare infinitive نوشت ('to write' or 'one should write'); together they express necessity.
segments.s728-g28: چو introduces a simile 'like/as'. جویی here is used in the sense of a flowing channel (a stream), not the lexical senses given for 'search'—no exact match in the provided options. پر از is the construction 'full of (something)'. می‌بباغ is an obscure/poetic compound in the manuscript and its exact reading/meaning is uncertain in the list; the whole phrase modifies how the letter should be written (a poetic simile). بهشت is 'paradise/garden'.
segments.s728-g29: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). با تو is a prepositional complement 'with you / toward you'. همی is an archaic/poetic particle that adds continuity or emphasis ('still/always' or 'will indeed'). دوستداری here functions as the notion 'friendship/affection' (not the modern 2nd-person verb form) and combines with کنم to mean 'I will show friendship / I will befriend you.' کنَم is 1st-person present/subjunctive used to express willingness or intention.
segments.s728-g30: بهر is a preposition 'for / toward / in order to' governing جای ('place') and the coordinated phrase هر کار ('every deed/task'). The verb phrase یاری کنم ('I will help') follows; the object of help (you) is implied from the prior با تو. کنم is again 1st-person present/subjunctive/intent marker.
segments.s728-g31: برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose/appeared/came'. برین is the older concatenation of بر + این ('upon/over this'); in context it introduces the temporal clause ('upon this / then'). روزگاری دراز is the temporal noun phrase 'a long time' serving as the complement of برآمد. The whole segment means 'a long time passed/ensued.'
segments.s728-g32: زبان here means 'speech/utterance' (the tongue as the speaker). بر دلم is a phrase 'upon my heart' (بر + دلم, where دلم = دل + enclitic -م 'my'). هیچ gives the negative/emphatic sense 'never / not at all' in this context. نگشاد is the negative past form 'did not open / did not disclose' (ن- prefix + گشاد). راز is the object 'secret.' Together: 'my tongue never disclosed the secret of my heart.'
segments.s728-g9: همی‌گفت is the iterative/imperfective past form (همی‑ + past) meaning 'kept saying' or 'was continually saying.' زین گونه is a two-word adverbial phrase: زین (here archaic/demonstrative) + گونه 'manner/way', so 'in this manner.' تا introduces a temporal clause 'until' and تیره گشت is a short clause where تیره ('gloomy/ominous') is the predicate adjective and گشت (past of گشتن) means 'became/turned.' The whole segment is: 'He kept saying in this way until it became grim.'
segments.s728-g10: ز here works as a causal/ablative preposition 'because of/from' (poetic use). گفتار is the noun 'speech/utterance' and چشم یلان is the possessive construction 'the eyes of the heroes' (adjective/noun + noun). خیره گشت is a two-word predicate where خیره ('fixed/staring/astonished') is an adjectival/predicative state and گشت 'became'; together 'their eyes became fixed' or 'were transfixed.' The phrase therefore means 'Because of his speech the heroes' eyes were transfixed.'
segments.s728-g11: چو at the start is a temporal conjunction 'when.' سازدندگان is a plural agent noun 'the makers/arrangers' (here those who set up/prepare), modified by the following noun شمع 'candle/taper' (so 'the candle‑makers/arrangers'). و می‌خواستند is the imperfective/past plural 'and they wanted' or 'and were about to,' linking this temporal clause to the main action that follows.
segments.s728-g12: همه 'all/whole' modifies کاخ 'palace/mansion' (whole palace). The item written ا appears in the manuscript as a separate character but here functions as an orthographic relic (object clitic or spelling irregularity) — no suitable sense supplied, so marked -1. ورا is the enclitic+pronoun 'to him/to her' (او را), and بیاراستند is the past plural verb 'they adorned/embellished.' Together: 'they adorned the whole palace for him.'
segments.s728-g13: ز is the preposition 'from/of' (poetic). بیگانه modifies مردم ('the foreign/strange people' or 'the people of foreign origin'), with adjective before noun as is common in Persian poetry. بپردخت is a past-tense verb (archaic spelling of پرداخت) meaning 'paid/gave/allocated' (here 'allocated/granted'), and جای is the noun 'place/seat/position' that is the object of that action. Literal structure: 'From the strangers/among the foreign people he allotted a place.'
segments.s728-g14: نشست here is the verbal past 'sat' (or 'took a seat'). از بر تخت is a compressed prepositional phrase: از (poetic/archaic connective) + بر 'on/upon' + تخت 'throne' — together indicating the locus 'on the throne' or motion relative to the throne. با introduces the instrument/companion 'with' and رهنمای is the noun 'guide/adviser.' So: 'he sat on the throne with a guide.'
segments.s728-g15: همان نیز is 'likewise/also that same.' گردوی here is a proper name (or a term whose poetic sense is not given in the provided senses) and is coordinated with خسرو by و 'and.' بهم is an adverb 'to one another/together' showing reciprocal action between the two names. Word order is typical: topic/adverbials first, then coordinated subjects.
segments.s728-g16: همی‌رفت is the iterative/imperfective past 'was going / kept moving' (habitual or iterative action). از گردیه uses از + گردیه (گردیه = 'assembly/gathering' or 'turning/gathered') to indicate origin or sphere 'from the gathering.' بیش و کم is an idiomatic pair 'more and less' meaning 'in greater or smaller numbers' or 'more or less.' The segment describes continual movement to/from the assembly in varying amounts.
segments.s728-g113: همان (demonstrative) precedes the noun to mark 'those same'; پنج تن is a counted noun phrase 'five persons' (تن = people/person). را is the Persian definite/direct-object marker attached after the object. بر خویش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/onto himself' (بر + خوی + ‑ش, where ش = his), and خواند is a past tense verb here with the poetic/archaic sense 'to call/summon' (not 'read'). Word order is Subject (implied) + object (marked by را) + prepositional phrase + verb.
segments.s728-g114: به نزدیکی خوابگه is a locative phrase: به + نزدیکی('nearness') + خوابگه('sleeping-place'), meaning 'near the sleeping-place' or 'to the vicinity of the bed/chamber'. برنشاند is a past transitive verb used in the sense 'to seat/place (someone)' — here it takes the previously-mentioned five men as its object (object is already marked by را earlier). The clause follows the common Persian order of locative phrase before the verb.
segments.s728-g115: چو ... شد is a temporal subordinating construction: چو = 'when' and شد = 'became' (past). شب تیره شد = 'when the night became dark.' روشنایی is the noun 'light/brightness' and بکشت is an archaic past verb here meaning 'caused to cease / put out' (a poetic usage; literally 'made/caused' in the supplied lemma but functioning as 'extinguished' in context). The object (روشنایی) precedes the verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s728-g116: This short clause is compressed/poetic. لب = 'lip' (or 'mouth' in figurative contexts). شوی is the archaic/poetic presentational form of شدن 'to become' (here forming a descriptive phrase). بگرفت is a past transitive 'he seized/took hold (of)'; ناگه = 'suddenly' modifies the action. بمشت is unclear/ambiguous in modern senses (see senses list); in context it conveys an abrupt violent action (a blow/strike or motion), but the provided lemma senses do not match that meaning directly.
segments.s728-g117: ازان is an older form of از آن 'from those/of those' and introduces the group. مردمان = 'people' (the men previously mentioned). نیز = 'also/too' adds inclusively. یار (here) = 'companion(s)' and آمدند is the past plural 'they came.' The clause literally reports that companions among those people also came.
segments.s728-g118: به بالین = 'to the bedside' (به + بالین, where بالین can mean 'by/at the pillow/bedside'). آن نامدار = 'that renowned/famed (man)' with آن as the demonstrative article and نامدار as adjective/noun 'renowned.' آمدند is the repeated past plural 'they came' (emphatic repetition typical of epic style). The prepositional phrase precedes the verb, giving location before motion.
segments.s728-g119: بکوشید is a past tense verb 'they tried/struggled' (کوشیدن). بسیار 'much/greatly' intensifies the verb. با + مرد مست = 'with the drunken man' (با = with; مرد = man/person; مست = drunk). The clause says they exerted great effort against the drunk man; word order is verb + adverb + prepositional phrase.
segments.s728-g120: سر انجام is an idiomatic compound meaning 'in the end/at last' (سر here combines with انجام 'result/outcome'). گویا is an adverb here meaning 'apparently/it seems.' زبانش ببست is a passive-like phrasing: زبان = 'tongue/speech', ش = 'his', and ببست (past form of بستن) = 'he/it closed' — together 'his tongue closed' i.e. 'his speech was stopped/he fell silent.' The clause thus reports the final outcome: apparently his tongue was shut.
segments.s728-g105: چو = "when" (temporal conjunction) + آن شیر زن = "that lion-woman" (demonstrative + compound noun) + نامه شاه = "the king's letter" (noun + possessive/genitive relationship, word order: possessee after possessor is common in poetry) + دید = perfect/past 'saw'. The whole phrase is a standard protasis: 'When that lion-woman saw the king's letter...'.
segments.s728-g106: تو گفتی بروی زمین ماه دید is reported speech. تو گفتی (here 'تو' = 'you' and گفتی would normally be 'you said' in modern Persian, but the lemma senses list lacked 'say' so that sense is marked separately) introduces what was said; بروی is an aorist/infinitive form meaning 'go' or 'went' (here past sense), زمین ماه دید is a verbless verblike clause 'saw (the) moon on the earth' — Persian poetry often strings verbs/objects compactly. Overall: 'she said, "you went and saw the moon on earth."'
segments.s728-g107: بخندید و گفت = 'she laughed and said' (past tense third person). این سخن = 'this utterance/remark'. رابه رنج = رابه (به + را fused: 'to it' or 'toward it' marking direction/target) + رنج ('suffering, grief'). Together: 'She laughed and said this remark (would cause) grief...' (the negative and completion appear in the following segment).
segments.s728-g108: ندارد کسی = lit. 'there is not/does not have anyone' — a negative existential construction meaning 'it will not affect anyone' or 'no one has (such) ...'. کش is an ambiguous, terse poetic element (here not matching the provided literal senses, so marked -1) and likely modifies یار پنج (either as an epithet or verbal idea). بود = past of 'to be'. یار پنج = 'the companion/ally of the five' (compound identifying membership). In context the clause denies that the remark will trouble anyone because the person was an ally of the five.
segments.s728-g109: بخواند = third-person past (he read/recited/summoned) — in epic poetry this past-tense narrative form is used. آن خط شاه = 'that (king's) letter/script' (آن = that, خط = handwriting/letter, شاه = king). بر پنج تن = 'to/upon the five persons' (بر with an animate plural 'پنج تن' indicates direction/recipient: 'to the five').
segments.s728-g110: نهان داشت = 'kept (it) hidden' (نهان = hidden/secret, داشت = had/kept — a common periphrastic perfect/past). زان = 'of that' / 'from that' (demonstrative or pronominal connector referring back to what was read or the affair). نامدار انجمن = 'renowned assembly/meeting' (epithet). Grammatically this is a compact past clause: 'he kept that renowned council hidden.'
segments.s728-g111: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction). بگشاد لب = a poetic past form 'she opened (her) lip' i.e. 'she began to speak' (بگشاد is an older/simple past). زود = adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying the following verb. پیمان ببست = 'she/they bound/sealed a covenant' (پیمان = pact, ببست = bound/fastened — past). The clause describes a quick action initiated at speech.
segments.s728-g112: گرفت آن زمان دست او را بدست = گرفت (he seized/took) + آن زمان (at that moment; آن = that, زمان = moment/time) + دست او را (her hand as definite object — را marks the object) + بدست (in the hand / into his hand). Word order places temporal phrase between verb and object; whole line: 'At that moment he took her hand in his.'
segments.s728-g73: پر از is the adjective phrase 'full of' (پر followed by the preposition از). The list that follows (عهد و پیوند و سوگندها) are plural nouns coordinated by و; they are the things the subject is 'full of'. Word order is typical Persian: 'پر از' + list of complements.
segments.s728-g74: ز هر گونه‌ای is a prepositional phrase 'of/from every kind' (ز used poetically for 'of'). گونه‌ای uses the -ای suffix to mean 'of a kind / of any kind'. لابه و پندها are two plural nouns joined by و and form a second group of complements to 'پر از' — 'pleas (لابه) and admonitions (پندها)'.
segments.s728-g75: چو here = 'when'. برگشت is a past-form verb meaning 'turned/backed' or 'was turned' (intransitive/passive sense here). عنوان آن نامه is a genitive phrase 'the heading/title of that letter', and خشک is an attributive adjective 'dry/stiff' modifying نامه — together: 'when the heading of that (dry) letter was turned back.'
segments.s728-g76: نهادند is 3rd-person plural past 'they placed/put'. مهری is the direct object 'a seal' (here indefinite/accusative form); برو appears as part of the noun phrase attached to the seal (it is not the verb 'go' here, but an element of the compound name/description and is lexically irregular/archaic). بر ز مشک is a prepositional stack 'upon, of/from musk' (بر = 'on', ز = 'of/from' in poetic order), so the whole means 'they placed a seal upon it made of musk.'
segments.s728-g77: نگینی is 'a signet' (a small gem or seal). برو again appears as part of the compound/name following نگینی and should be read as an attached element (not the verb 'go'). نام پرویز شاه is an appositive/genitive construction 'the name “Perviz Shah”' that specifies what the signet bears.
segments.s728-g78: نهادند 'they placed' (same past 3pl). بر مهر مشک سیاه is the prepositional phrase 'on/onto the seal of black musk' (بر = 'on', مهر = 'seal', مشک = 'musk' modifying مهر, سیاه = 'black' describing مشک). Word order groups the modifiers after the head noun in the classical poetic style.
segments.s728-g79: یکی نامه بنوشت is an archaic/poetic way to say 'he wrote a (certain) letter' (بنوشت = past 'wrote' in older usage). گردوی follows as a postpositive adjective 'walnut-colored' describing the letter (classical Persian often places adjectives after nouns). نیز 'also' links this letter to what preceded.
segments.s728-g80: بگفت here functions like 'he wrote/said (in it)' — in classical usage بگفت + اندرو means 'in it he wrote/said'. اندرو = اندر + او 'in it/within that'. پند و بسیار چیز is the coordinated object 'advice and many things' (پند = 'advice', بسیار = 'many' here, and چیز = 'things').
segments.s728-g33: کنون (now) is an adverb at the front for temporal emphasis. روزگار (time/age) + سخن گفتن (to speak) forms the noun-phrase ‘the time for speaking’; گفتن is the infinitive/masdar used with است (copula) to state existence ('it is the time to speak'). Est (است) is the present copula.
segments.s728-g34: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause here ('for' / 'because'). گردوی ما is a genitival phrase ('our walnut'); رابجای is a fused prepositional phrase meaning 'in place of' (را marks the following noun as definite/object), and تنست is literally 'is (a) body' (here: 'stands in place of the body'). The clause explains the reason it is now proper to speak.
segments.s728-g35: نگر is an imperative ('look', 'consider'). تا here introduces the purpose/result clause ('so/that'), followed by چگونه کنی ('how you should/do'), where کنی is the present subjunctive/imperative 2nd-person form of کردن used to express potential action; چاره‌ای is the object 'a remedy/means'.
segments.s728-g36: کزان is a compound (کِ + زان) meaning 'from which' or 'by which' and links this clause to the prior 'چاره' (i.e., 'a remedy by which...'). گم (lost) is an adjective/predicative; شود is the present/future auxiliary 'becomes/will be' used here to form a potential/result ('will become lost'); زشت qualifies پتیاره‌ای ('an ugly/unsightly پتیاره', the final noun is archaic/obscure).
segments.s728-g37: که again introduces a subordinate clause ('that/so that'). گستهم appears as a nominalized form referring to the speaker's wandering/spread (here treated as an object) and is marked by را. زیر is the preposition 'under', and سنگ‌آوری is a present/derived compound meaning 'bringing (under) a stone' or 'placing under a stone' (آوری is incorporated as part of the compound).
segments.s728-g38: دل و خانه ما lists two direct objects ('heart and house of ours') with ما as the possessive; به here marks direction/into ('into your...'), چنگ is used metaphorically as 'grasp/hand/claw' (not the musical harp in this context), and آوری is the 2nd/3rd-person present of آوردن ('you bring' / 'you seize'), continuing the purposive/resultive sense begun by که.
segments.s728-g39: چو = 'when' introduces a temporal condition. این is the demonstrative 'this'; کرده باشی is a compound perfect/subjunctive ('having done' / 'when you have done') with کرده (past participle) + باشی (subjunctive of بودن) to mark completed action. سپاه تو را places 'your army' (سپاه + possessive تو) with را as a topic/emphatic marker.
segments.s728-g40: همان (= 'that very one') refers back to سپاه; در جهان ('in the world') is a locative phrase modifying the following predicate; نیک‌خواه تو را is a nominal phrase 'your well-wisher' with را again marking emphasis/definiteness. The verb copula is omitted (understood), so the clause reads '[it will be] that in the world your well-wisher.'
segments.s728-g41: مر is an archaic dative/benefactive ('to/for me' or 'for my part') placed at the line's start for emphasis. آن را که خواهی is a relative clause 'that which you want' (آن را = 'that thing' + که خواهی = 'which you desire' with خواهی = 2nd‑person present/future 'you want/will'). دهم (implicit in the segment's surface) is the 1st‑person present/future verb 'I give / I will give', and کشوری (a noun with adjectival/partitive -ی) is the thing being given 'a kingdom/realm' — note the poetic word order places the object after the verb phrase.
segments.s728-g42: بگردد is a 3rd‑person present/subjunctive form of گردیدن ('to turn, to rule, to occur'); here it functions like 'let it/one rule' or 'may it be turned/placed'. بر ... کشور = 'over/on that country' (بر = 'on/upon', آن کشور = 'that country'). اندر سری is an adverbial phrase 'in secret/secretly' (اندر = 'in/within', سری = 'secret/hidden'). The clause as a whole means something like 'let a ruler be placed on that land in secret' or 'may one secretly rule over that country.'
segments.s728-g43: توآیی is the enclitic/poetic 2nd‑person form 'thou art/you come' used addressingly; here it identifies the addressee as the subject. به marks manner or accompaniment ('in'/'with') and مشکوی زرین is a compound adjectival phrase 'musk‑scented, golden' modifying a (possibly elided) noun like 'garment' or 'robe' that the speaker associates with themselves: من is the speaker's pronoun used possessively in context ('my'). The phrase packs descriptive detail before the predicate.
segments.s728-g44: سرآورده باشی is a verbal phrase where سرآورده (literally 'that which has been brought about' or 'the doer/causer') combines with باشی (2nd‑person present/subjunctive/auxiliary) to form a perfective/resultant idea: 'you have caused' / 'you have brought upon'. همه کین من = 'all my hatred' (همه = 'all', کین = 'hatred/vengeance', من = 'my/I'). So the clause says that the addressee has become the cause/agent of all the speaker's hatred.
segments.s728-g45: برین is the demonstrative/integrative particle (بر + ین, here ین = orthographic/poetic variant of 'این') meaning 'on this' or 'thereon'. برخورم is 1st‑person subjunctive/present 'I meet/encounter/lay (my hand) on' and in oath formulas functions as 'I take (an oath) upon this'. سخت سوگند is an emphatic collocation 'a solemn/firm oath' (سخت here adverbially/qualifying the oath), and نیز = 'also'. Together the clause is an oath formula: 'Upon this I take a solemn oath as well.'
segments.s728-g46: فزایم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive of فزودن 'to increase/add' — here 'I will add' or 'I add'. برین again 'to/on this', بندها 'the stanzas/verses' (plural) and بند (repeated) emphasize 'another stanza/verse' (a common poetical self‑reference in classical Persian: adding another couplet). نیز 'also/too' closes the line. The clause states the poet's intention to add an additional stanza to what precedes.
segments.s728-g47: اگر begins a conditional 'if'. پیچم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive of پیچیدن 'to turn/twist' used reflexively here 'if I turn/waver' (i.e., if I change my heart). این دل = 'this heart' (direct object). ز سوگند من = 'from my oath' (ز = 'from/because of', سوگند = 'oath', من = speaker's pronoun used possessively). The clause is a protasis: 'If I turn this heart away from my oath...'.
segments.s728-g48: مبادا is a negative optative/formula meaning 'may it not be' or 'lest' (used to forbid/wish against something). ز من = 'from me' (or 'because of me'), شاد = 'happy/joyful', پیوند من = 'my bond/connection' (پیوند = 'bond, tie'); syntactically this is a protective wish: 'May my bond not be a cause of joy for me' — i.e. 'may it not be that I find joy because I broke my oath.' The line uses short phrases and elliptic ordering typical of classical Persian supplicatory formulas.
segments.s728-g17: "بدو گفت" is a two-word sequence: بدو is a prepositional-like clitic meaning 'to him/unto him' and گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (3rd person). "ز ایدر فراوان سپاه" follows: ز + ایدر = 'from here' (poetic order), and فراوان سپاه = 'a plentiful/large army' (adjective placed before the noun for poetic emphasis). The whole segment functions as the speech-introducer: 'he said to him, from here a large army...'.
segments.s728-g18: به آمل فرستاده‌ام is a perfect construction: فرستاده‌ام is the past-participle + 1st-singular clitic meaning 'I have sent', and به آمل = 'to Amol'. کینه خواه is a descriptive purpose/participial phrase — here split into two words for poetic effect — meaning 'seeking vengeance' or 'as vengeance-seeking'; خواه is used in the sense 'to want/seek' so together they express purpose ('to exact revenge').
segments.s728-g19: همه is the subject 'all/everyone'. خسته و کُشته are two coordinated predicative adjectives/participles ('weary and slain'); بازآمدند is the past plural verb 'they returned/they came back'. The sequence states the condition of the returning group: 'all returned, weary and slain.'
segments.s728-g20: پرازناله is a compound adjective/adverb meaning 'full of lamentation' (پر + از + ناله compressed in poetry). وبا here functions as an affliction/affective noun 'scourge/affliction' paired with گداز (a noun meaning 'burning/ardor' used metaphorically for anguish). آمدند repeats the verb 'they came' and ties the descriptive phrases back to the subject. Together: 'they came full of lamentation and with burning anguish.'
segments.s728-g21: کنون = 'now'. اندرین = 'among these' (an archaic/poetic prepositional form). رای ما را literally 'our counsel/opinion' with را marking the phrase as definite/topic. یکیست = 'there is one' (one [counsel] exists). The whole segment sets up a single chosen plan among several: 'Now among these counsels of ours there is one.'
segments.s728-g22: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the content of the one counsel. از رای ما = 'by/from our counsel' (از = 'from/by' indicating source or basis). تاج و تخت = 'crown and throne' (a merism for sovereignty). اندکیست is an existential/predicative form 'there is little' / 'is of little worth'; together the clause means 'that, by our counsel, crown and throne are of little account.' Note the somewhat compressed/poetic agreement (اندکیست behaves as a general predicative comment on the preceding nouns).
segments.s728-g23: چو = 'when'. بهرام چوبینه is the personal name with epithet (Chubin); in poetry the epithet appears adjacent to the name. گم کرد is the past construction 'lost' (کرد used with the participle گم), and راه = 'the way/path'. The clause reads 'When Bahram Chubin lost the way...' with چو introducing the temporal clause.
segments.s728-g24: همیشه = 'always'. بدی = 'evil' (noun). گردیه is a past-participial/poetic form of گردیدن 'turned/become', here meaning 'has turned' or 'became'. نیک‌خواه = 'well-wisher' (one who wishes well). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: the sense is 'the well-wisher has always turned to evil' (i.e. even well-wishers prove harmful).
segments.s728-g1: چنین تا برآمد برین چندگاه is a temporal clause: چنین 'thus/so' sets the tone; تا here means 'until/after' (a temporal marker); برآمد is the verbal kernel 'arose/passed' and is used idiomatically with بر این (here written together as برین) + چندگاه to mean 'when a few days had come/passed'. Word order is poetic (adverb + temporal marker + verb + locution).
segments.s728-g2: ز گستهم پر درد شد جان شاه is the main clause: ز here means 'because of' or 'from'; گستهم is a first‑person verbal form used nominally ('my wandering/I having wandered'); پر درد is a predicate adjective phrase 'full (پر) of pain/sorrow (درد)'; شد is the perfective/become verb 'became'; جان شاه ('the king's life/soul') is the affected subject. The literal order is cause + predicate + copula + subject (poetic inversion).
segments.s728-g3: برآشفت روزی به گردوی گفت is a clause of reporting with a temporal adverb: برآشفت 'became upset/angry' is the main verb; روزی here functions as a temporal adverb 'one day' (poetic use); به marks the indirect object/recipient 'to'; گردوی is the addressee's name (poetically written). The final گفت is the reporting verb introducing the following content clause ('said that...').
segments.s728-g4: که گستهم با گردیه گشت جفت is the content clause introduced by که 'that'. گستهم is the first‑person root ('my wandering' / 'I had wandered') and serves as subject/agent; با + noun expresses accompaniment ('with Gordiyeh'); گشت is the verbal 'became/turned' and here takes جفت ('pair') as its predicate complement, so 'became a pair' or 'was joined as a match'. The clause reports that the speaker's wandering had become joined with that person.
segments.s728-g5: سوی او شدند آن بزرگ انجمن is a fronted verbal clause in poetic order: سوی او شدند literally 'turned toward him/approached him' (سوی + pronoun + شدند); the subject follows the verb: آن بزرگ انجمن 'that great assembly/meeting'. The verb شدند is the past plural of شدن used here as 'turned/moved' or 'came' toward someone.
segments.s728-g6: برانم که او بودشان رای زن is an utterance clause introduced by که 'that'. The form برانم is an archaic/poetic verb-form here introducing what the assembly said or declared (its precise etymology is irregular in the manuscript tradition); او 'she/he' is the topical pronoun, and بودشان is a past‑form construction meaning 'was to them' or 'was theirs' (i.e. 'she was theirs'). رای زن is a compound 'rāy‑zan' meaning 'counsel‑woman' or 'their counsellor/advisor' (رای 'advice' + زن 'woman'). Grammatically, بودشان (past copula + enclitic pronoun) links the pronoun او to the nominal predicate 'رای زن'.
segments.s728-g7: از آمل کس آمد ز کارآگهان is a nominal + relative description: از آمل کس آمد 'a person came from Amol' (از + place + کس 'someone' + آمد 'came'). The following small phrase ز کارآگهان qualifies that person: ز here is 'from/about' in poetic register and کارآگهان (from کارآگه) means 'one who is informed/knowledgeable' or 'an informer/one who knows', so the whole segment reads 'someone from Amol, an informed man, came.'
segments.s728-g8: همه فاش کرد آنچ بودی نهان is the reporting action: همه فاش کرد 'he revealed all' where همه is the object 'all' and فاش کرد is the verb 'made open/revealed'. آنچ بودی نهان is a relative clause 'that which had been hidden' (آنچ 'that which' + بودی, past of بودن, 'had been' + نهان 'hidden'), so the whole means 'he openly disclosed everything that had been concealed.'
segments.s728-g49: بدو گفت is a short clause meaning “he said to him”: بدو is the prepositional dative ‘to him’, and گفت is the past tense verb ‘said’ (classical wording; the lemma senses list did not include the verbal sense). گردوی نوشه is a noun phrase modifying what was given: گردوی here describes the drink as ‘walnut‑coloured/like a walnut’ and نوشه is ‘a draught/sip’ — together ‘a walnut‑coloured draught’. The final بدی in the segment is the archaic 2nd‑person past of دادن (‘you gave’), not the nominal senses listed, and ties the clause as reported speech: “you gave …”.
segments.s728-g50: چو here introduces a comparison/temporal clause meaning ‘when’ or ‘as’. ناهید is the planet/goddess Venus, در برج is the locative phrase ‘in the sign/house (of the zodiac)’ (در = in; برج = zodiac sign here), and خوشه is ‘a cluster/bunch’ (e.g. of grapes). The final بدی again is the 2nd‑person past ‘you gave’ linking this clause to the previous statement (so: ‘as when Venus in her sign gave a cluster’).
segments.s728-g51: تو دانی که is a standard clause introducer: تو = ‘you’, دانی = ‘know’, که = ‘that’ introducing the content clause. من جان و فرزند خویش is the content: من = ‘I’, جان = ‘my life/soul’, و فرزند = ‘and (my) children/offspring’. خوی + ش is an older way to form the possessive ‘my/one’s own’: خوی here (kin/relationship) plus the enclitic ش marks possession (referring back to من).
segments.s728-g52: برو here functions as the verb ‘go’ (first person sense implied by the clause’s subject earlier or a hortative/intent: ‘I will go’/‘I go’). بوم آباد is a compound meaning ‘prosperous/inhabited homeland’ (بوم = homeland, آباد = prosperous/settled). و پیوند خویش ties the clause to ‘my family/bonds’: پیوند = ‘bond, tie’, خوی + ش again yields the possessive ‘my kin/family bond’. Word order places the goal/location after the verb, as typical in Persian.
segments.s728-g53: بجای سر تو ندارم is a negative possession construction: بجای = ‘in place of/instead of’, سر تو = ‘your head’ (سر = head), and ندارم = ‘I do not have’ (present tense with negative prefix ن‑). The phrase means literally ‘I have nothing instead of your head’ (i.e. no substitute). The following به چیز makes explicit that no ‘thing’ exists to replace it.
segments.s728-g54: This sentence-ender qualifies or concedes the previous claim. چیزها = ‘things’ (plural). ارجمندست is a predicative adjective ‘is/are honorable, precious’ (‑ست is the copula ‘is’), and نیز = ‘also/yet’. The initial گرین is an archaic/poetic modifier here (not represented in the provided sense list) functioning as an emphatic or demonstrative; the whole line reads ‘those (or such) things are also precious.’
segments.s728-g55: بدین کس is the dative phrase ‘to/for this man’ (classical spelling: به + این collapsed), where the data split the piecewise forms (ب = to; دین here corresponds to ‘this’ but its sense wasn’t in the list). فرستم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive or jussive of فرستادن (‘I will/send I send’ in classical register). به نزدیک اوی means ‘to near him / to his presence’ (به = to, نزدیک = near, اوی = his/her — here ‘his’). Together: ‘I will send this man to be near him.’
segments.s728-g56: درفشان is used adjectivally here as ‘shining, glittering’ (literally a banner‑shaker or ‘radiant’ in poetic use). کنم is the 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive of کردن ‘I make/do’, so درفشان کنم جان تاریک اوی = ‘I will make his dark life shine’ (جان = life/soul, تاریک = dark/obscure, اوی = his). The object (جان تاریک اوی) follows the verb complex as in Persian.

sentences.s728-s49: She was charging/rushing up to the elm grove; the woman's messenger came near the woman.
sentences.s728-s50: From her there turned/appeared, like joyful spring, that same face full of scent and color and beauty.
sentences.s728-s51: They spoke a little on behalf of Bahram; they still sprinkled the water of their eyelashes (they shed tears).
sentences.s728-s52: Then that letter, in the king's hand/script, she secretly gave to him and showed him the way.
sentences.s728-s45: When my mate comes near you, he will make your dark soul shine.
sentences.s728-s46: Turn not from his words in any way; when, like dust, fortune's face grows pale.
sentences.s728-s47: He placed that royal line in the midst and wrapped the plan in silk.
sentences.s728-s48: The resourceful woman tied up that letter; she heard those words that satisfied her.

sentences.s728-s42: May the generous God’s forgiveness be upon you; do not be regretful of that conversation.

sentences.s728-s44: If he departs, we will go after him; we will turn to (appeal to) the God of the world.
sentences.s728-s29: I will send a single note under the king's seal; his very handwriting there is like the shining moon.
sentences.s728-s30: I will send my wife to that contemptible person, and I will drive the ill-intentioned man far from this court.
sentences.s728-s31: For such talk is nothing but a woman's affair, especially a woman whose opinion was that of women.
sentences.s728-s32: And whenever I look upon this as well, your message ought to go to my sister.
sentences.s728-s33: May this matter soon turn out to your liking; on this one should not add either more or less.
sentences.s728-s34: When Khosrow heard that, he became glad; all his sufferings were blown from his heart.
sentences.s728-s35: Then he also asked for paper from the treasury; he asked for (an item) from the black musk Sudeh.
sentences.s728-s36: He wrote a letter; like a garden — the garden's flowers like the faces of friends.
sentences.s728-s65: Then the king showed them that letter and increased their courage and swiftness.
sentences.s728-s66: All the headstrong ones cried 'Bravo!' and scattered jewels upon that letter.
sentences.s728-s61: The spahbed died in the darkness, and (when) day became clear he was handed over to Khosrow.
sentences.s728-s62: Within the city cries and shouts rose up; toward/against a woman fire and wind sprang up.
sentences.s728-s63: When the woman heard the cry, the (woman) of pure conduct put on a Roman sleeping-garment for her body.
sentences.s728-s64: On the dark night he summoned the Iranians; he spoke for a while of the slain.
sentences.s728-s13: Now there is a solution close to me; do not speak this matter before the assembly.
sentences.s728-s14: One must write a letter to the assembly, like a stream full of wine toward the garden of paradise.
sentences.s728-s15: That I will be your friend (love you) and will help you in every place and in every task.
sentences.s728-s16: A long time then came (passed); my tongue never opened the secret of my heart.
sentences.s728-s5: He kept speaking in this way until the tone grew grim; at his words the heroes' eyes were transfixed.
sentences.s728-s6: When the candle‑arrangers wanted (to do it), they adorned the whole palace for him.
sentences.s728-s7: From the strangers the people allotted a place; he sat upon the throne with a guide.
sentences.s728-s8: Likewise, Gerdoy and Khosrow drew together toward one another, coming and going from the assembly in greater and lesser numbers.
sentences.s728-s57: He summoned those same five men to himself and set them down near the sleeping-place.
sentences.s728-s58: When the night grew dark he extinguished the light; suddenly he seized (a mouth/lip) and struck.
sentences.s728-s59: From those people, too, companions came; they came to the bedside of that renowned man.
sentences.s728-s60: They strove greatly with the drunken man; at last, it seems, his tongue was silenced.
sentences.s728-s53: When that lion-woman saw the king's letter, she said, "You went out and saw the moon on the earth."
sentences.s728-s54: She laughed and said that this remark would bring no one grief — he was a companion of the five.
sentences.s728-s55: He read that royal letter to the five, and he kept that famed assembly hidden.
sentences.s728-s56: When she opened her lips, she quickly sealed the pact; at that moment he took her hand in his.
sentences.s728-s37: It was full of oaths, bonds, and vows, and of every kind — pleas and admonitions.
sentences.s728-s38: When the heading of that letter was turned back, they set a seal upon it of musk.
sentences.s728-s39: They set a signet on it bearing the name 'Perviz Shah' upon a seal of black musk.
sentences.s728-s40: He wrote another letter, walnut-colored; in it he wrote advice and many other things.
sentences.s728-s17: Now is the time to speak, for our walnut stands in the place of the body.
sentences.s728-s18: Consider how you can devise a remedy by which that ugly patiyāra will be lost.
sentences.s728-s19: So that you may lay my wandering under a stone, and take our heart and house into your grasp.
sentences.s728-s20: When you have done this, your army will be your well-wisher in the world.
sentences.s728-s21: Whatever you desire I will give (you) — a kingdom; let a ruler secretly reign over that land.
sentences.s728-s22: You — in my golden, musk‑scented (garment) — have brought upon me all my hatred.
sentences.s728-s23: On this I take a solemn oath; I will likewise add another stanza to these stanzas.
sentences.s728-s24: If I should turn this heart away from my oath, may there not be joy for me in my bonds (may my tie not be a joyful one).
sentences.s728-s9: He said to him, “From here I have sent a large army to Amol to exact revenge.”
sentences.s728-s10: They all came back, weary and slain; they returned full of lamentation and burning anguish.
sentences.s728-s11: Now, among these counsels of ours there is one: that, by our counsel, crown and throne are of little account.
sentences.s728-s12: When Bahram Chubin lost the way, the well-wisher always turned to evil.
sentences.s728-s1: Thus, when a few days had passed, the king's life became full of sorrow because of my wandering.
sentences.s728-s2: One day he grew angry and said to Gerdouy that my wandering had become paired with Gordiyeh.
sentences.s728-s3: That great assembly turned to him and said that she had been their counsellor.
sentences.s728-s4: From Amol a man came — an informed one — and he revealed openly all that had been hidden.
sentences.s728-s25: He said to him, “You gave a walnut‑coloured draught; as Venus in her sign gave a cluster.”
sentences.s728-s26: You know that I — my life and my offspring — (look to) my prosperous homeland and my family ties.
sentences.s728-s27: I have nothing to offer in place of your head; yet those things are precious as well.
sentences.s728-s28: I will send this man to be near him; I will make his dark life shine.
